name: Urban Grill (Mostar old town)
description: Budget grill in the old bazaar (~150m north of the bridge) doing cevapi, mixed grill (mjesano meso) and wood-fired burek on a terrace over the Radobolja. The local-leaning alternative to the two famous tourist names.
communityNotes: Repeatedly flagged on TripAdvisor as 'a recommendation from locals' with 'flavorful, nicely priced cevapi'; the mixed-meat grill is called 'a dish favoured by locals' and the burek 'wood-fired with a blackened top'. Consistent 'great food + great view + reasonable rates' theme.
bestTime: Lunch; terrace fills at midday in June

name: Cevabdzinica Tima-Irma (Mostar)
description: The most-hyped cevapi/grill house in Mostar, a 30-year family business on Onescukova in the old town. Huge portions; famous and therefore touristy.
communityNotes: Verdict is MIXED, not a slam-dunk. Many love it ('amazing cevapi with ajvar and kajmak') but recurring complaints about long waits, theatrical slow service, and one reviewer said the cevapi were 'just okay compared to Sarajevo'. Worth it but go early and expect a queue — not a hidden local secret.
bestTime: Before 12:00 or after 14:00 to dodge the queue

name: Restoran Hindin Han (Mostar)
description: Traditional Bosnian restaurant on a terrace over the Radobolja by the Crooked Bridge — the best sit-down dinner spot of the bazaar restaurants (veal in sauce, fresh fish, cheese/meat boards).
communityNotes: Top-10 of Mostar restaurants on TripAdvisor: 'cosy and relaxed atmosphere, amazing sound of the river underneath'; 'dreaming about those cheeses, meats and breads for the rest of my life'. A few note seating crunch / inconsistent service at peak. The 'proper meal' counterpoint to grab-and-go cevapi.
bestTime: Dinner / golden hour on the river terrace

name: Stari Most jumpers viewpoint (west-bank terrace)
description: The west-bank Halebija tower holds a small cafe, the Mostar Diving Club and two riverside observation decks below it — where travelers say you get front-row views of the bridge jumpers without being on the bridge.
communityNotes: Trip reports converge: 'a little cafe occupies the tower at the west bank — best place to see the divers fly'; 'the two observation decks below the bridge are the best spot for photographing the jumps — find your way down through the cafes at the western end'. Jumpers only go once enough tips are collected, so expect a wait.
cost: Free (drink if you sit at the cafe)
bestTime: Late morning to afternoon in summer = most jump activity

name: Black Dog Pub (Mostar)
description: Craft-beer pub on the Radobolja by the Crooked Bridge — the go-to traveler/backpacker drinking spot in the old town, 7+ rotating beers including local craft, frequent live music.
communityNotes: Well-liked: 'spent hours making friends with locals', 'huge beer selection, 3-7 KM', 'live music, cool atmosphere, affordable'. Caveats: no printed menu/prices and occasional sharp service. Net: the best old-town pub for the river setting + beer range.
bestTime: After dark; live-music nights

name: Ali Baba cave bar / club (Mostar)
description: A bar/club literally inside a natural cave in the rock by the old bazaar, entered through a tunnel; the downstairs 'Cave' is the late-night club. The most distinctive nightlife venue in Mostar.
communityNotes: The unique-setting pick: 'went with hostel friends to The Cave (the club part downstairs) — great fun, good music and atmosphere'. Novelty of drinking inside a cave is the draw; gets busy/sweaty late.
bestTime: After ~22:00

name: Restoran Vrelo (Blagaj, on the Buna)
description: Riverside restaurant in Blagaj with the best terrace view of the Tekija dervish house and Buna spring — trout/fresh fish and Bosnian dishes, or just a Bosnian coffee over the emerald water. 20 min from Mostar.
communityNotes: 'Restoran Vrelo had the best view of the Dervish House from their terrace — Bosnian coffee or a traditional lunch, the perfect lazy afternoon.' General Blagaj advice repeated everywhere: come before ~10:30 or after ~16:00 — by noon in June it is crowded with tour groups.
bestTime: Before 10:30 or after 16:00 to beat the tour buses

name: Kocusa Waterfall (Trebizat)
description: Wide (50m+) low travertine cascade on the Trebizat near Ljubuski with an old working watermill beside it — the quiet, FREE, swimmable alternative to crowded Kravica, ~20 km away.
communityNotes: The crowd contrast is the headline: travelers report 'the same day in late May, ~200 people at Kravica vs just 5 at Kocusa'. Free, rustic, pebbled pool from ankle-deep to 2-3m for swimming, and the watermill makes the standout photo. Most bus tours skip it — self-drive only.
bestTime: May-June fullest flow; weekday mornings empty

name: Auto Camp Ante (Kravica)
description: Small family-run campsite ~700m from Kravica falls — the practical base for hitting the waterfall at dawn before the crowds and the ticket window. Modern sanitary block, BBQ area.
communityNotes: Strong park4night/Campercontact reviews: 'lovely clean and remote, new and immaculate toilets and showers, hot water and great WiFi'; 'very welcoming hosts, German and English speaking'; 'planned a couple of nights, stayed six'. The known trick: arrive at the falls outside the 07:00-19:00 ticket window and it is free + empty.
cost: ~€15-20/night car + tent + 2 (confirm 4-person rate on arrival); separate Kravica entry €10/adult if entering 07:00-19:00
bestTime: June; book ahead — it is small
facilities: Modern hot showers + toilets, electricity, BBQ area, WiFi, reception shop, shaded/riverside

name: Sarajevo (Bascarsija old town)
description: Bosnia's capital and the trip's one real city stop. Bascarsija is the 15th-c. Ottoman bazaar — Sebilj fountain, coppersmiths' alley, Gazi Husrev-beg Mosque, and the 'Meeting of Cultures' line where the bazaar abruptly becomes an Austro-Hungarian boulevard. Compact, cheap, walkable, with genuine nightlife.
communityNotes: Travelers call Bascarsija 'one of the best places in the city to locate yourself' for old-town atmosphere — a working bazaar of coppersmiths and cafes. The east-west cultural divide and dense cevapi/cafe scene are the repeated highlights. Best cevapi at Zeljo or Petica; nightlife around Ferhadija (Silver & Smoke, Sloga, Pink Houdini jazz, craft-beer bars) is a decisive step up from Mostar for a night out.
cost: Apartments for 4 ~€50/night achievable (one of the cheaper capitals); hostels cheaper
timeNeeded: Half day on foot covers the core; overnight recommended
bestTime: Early evening for the buzz; mornings calmer in June

name: Tunnel of Hope (Sarajevo War Tunnel Museum)
description: The 800m tunnel hand-dug under the airport runway that was Sarajevo's only lifeline during the 1992-96 siege. The museum (the house whose cellar was the southern mouth) preserves ~20-25m of tunnel to walk, plus photos, artifacts and a short film. Out by the airport (SW), not the old town.
communityNotes: Consistently top-rated: 'our favourite site in Sarajevo'; 'really worth visiting... gives you the inside and practical information on how Sarajevo and its citizens lived during the war'; 'almost an obligatory activity even for mainstream tourists'. Slot it on arrival or departure since you drive through that side anyway. ⚡ GROUP FIT NOTE: History/museum-focused — genuinely powerful if anyone in the group is interested in 20th-century conflict; skip-able if the Trebević cable car + bobsled track + Baščaršija night better fits the energy. Competing with a limited Sarajevo day.
cost: 20 KM adult flat (~€10) — cash KM only, no cards, no euros (plan a Sarajevo ATM stop first)
timeNeeded: 45-60 min on your own; half-day with a guided war tour
bestTime: Apr-Oct 08:30-17:30, last entry 17:00; go early to beat tour groups

name: Cevabdzinica Zeljo (Sarajevo)
description: Sarajevo cevapi institution since 1968 (two facing branches on Kundurdziluk in Bascarsija) — the name most often dropped as the locals' favourite. Cevapi in somun bread with raw onion and kajmak, cash only.
communityNotes: Local guides list the three locals name as 'Zeljo, Mrkva and Petica Ferhatovic', with Zeljo singled out as 'a locals favourite'. Reviewers: 'probably best cevapi in Old Town', fast service, central. The eternal Zeljo-vs-Petica debate is 'Beatles vs Rolling Stones, only with more onions' — both are the real deal.
bestTime: Lunch; busy and turns over fast

name: Battle of Sutjeska Monument (Tjentiste)
description: Massive brutalist WWII memorial — two 19m concrete wings on a hillside marking the 1943 Battle of Sutjeska. The valley's centerpiece, a 2-minute detour off the M20, and the easiest possible stop on a transit day toward the Scepan Polje border.
communityNotes: Named one of GQ's '9 Brutalist Wonders'; visitors describe the scale and quiet as 'an otherworldly experience'. Reached by semicircular stairs (200+ steps), some 'badly maintained' in places. Right by the road as you leave Tjentiste south. Safety note (Spomenik DB): the monument and roads are fine but landmines remain in parts of the wider park — stay on marked paths.
cost: Monument free; small fee for the Spomen-Dom museum
bestTime: Anytime; great light late afternoon

name: Hotel Mladost & Kamp Sutjeska (Tjentiste)
description: The practical place to sleep at the heart of Sutjeska: a basic communist-era hotel plus an adjacent riverside campsite, right by the monument and the only real cluster of services (2 restaurants, 1 small shop) in the park.
communityNotes: Hotel Mladost is the de-facto base: 'probably the best place to base yourself for Sutjeska... basic by American standards but will more than suffice', 'staff super helpful, rooms comfortable with good bathrooms', big outdoor pool. Kamp Sutjeska sits next to it 'by the river with several barbecue areas' (~8.3 recent rating) but an older review trashed the sanitary block — check current state. Buy ALL food before arriving: 'learn from our mistakes and bring supplies'.
cost: Hotel Mladost: budget 2-star (cheap); Kamp Sutjeska: small per-tent fee; wild camping at Prijevor/Trnovacko free
bestTime: Book the hotel ahead in June; stock up on food first
facilities: Hotel: rooms, restaurant, large pool. Camp: riverside pitches, BBQ, toilets/showers (quality variable)

name: Trebinje Old Town (Kastel) & Plane Tree Square
description: Walled Ottoman old town (Kastel) on the Trebisnjica, opening onto a square shaded by 100-year-old plane trees full of cheap cafes and konobas. The standout cheap base near Dubrovnik: ~30-40 min inland but a fraction of the price, with a real wine-town vibe.
communityNotes: 'The easiest cross-border day trip out of Dubrovnik, just thirty kilometres on a quiet inland road'; 'accommodation, restaurants and services are 30-50% more affordable than Dubrovnik'. One Rick Steves poster was blunt: 'there's not much to the town' — its value is base + wine + bridge + monastery viewpoint + cheap nights + the Saturday green market, not blockbuster sights. The square 'gets pretty hectic once the sun drops' (bars/pubs aplenty).
cost: Apartments for 4 ~€30-70/night (cheap hotels avg ~€45-50); Numbeo: Dubrovnik rent ~272% higher
timeNeeded: Half day to see; 1-2 nights as a base
bestTime: Evening for the square; Saturday AM for the market. June books up early.

name: Vukoje Winery (Podrumi Vukoje 1982, Trebinje)
description: Trebinje's flagship winery on the edge of town, a multi-storey building topped by a terrace bar/restaurant overlooking the city. Tours + tastings of the native Zilavka (white) and Vranac (red); among the best-rated wineries in SE Europe.
communityNotes: TripAdvisor 4.6/5: 'a do-not-miss experience if you're ever in Trebinje. The wine was really good, the food delicious, service 5 stars.' Tip from a reviewer: 'ask about the per-glass tasting, otherwise they bring a full bottle each time.' The rooftop terrace is the selling point: 'lunch and wine tasting on the terrace with a view of Trebinje is the most beautiful thing.'
cost: Tasting + meal varies; ask for per-glass tastings to keep it cheap
bestTime: Sunset on the rooftop terrace; book ahead in summer
facilities: Terrace bar + restaurant, guided tours, shop

name: Tvrdos Monastery (wine-making monks)
description: 15th-century Serbian Orthodox monastery ~6 km west of Trebinje among vineyards, where monks make award-winning Vranac and Zilavka in a cellar partly dating to the 1400s (their Grand Reserve Vranac won Decanter gold in 2018). Grounds + shop free to wander; the setting is the draw.
communityNotes: 'Real wine tasting!' and a frequent highlight of Trebinje-area trips. Important booking caveat repeated by visitors: 'you need an organized tour or to contact them directly weeks in advance to arrange a tasting. You can walk the grounds and shop in the store but will not be able to taste the wine without arrangements.' Buy bottles in the shop even without a tasting.
cost: Free to visit grounds/shop; tasting must be pre-arranged (or via tour)
bestTime: Daytime; arrange any tasting weeks ahead
facilities: Monastery shop (wine, honey, rakija, olive oil), grounds

name: Hercegovacka Gracanica (Trebinje monastery viewpoint)
description: Serbian Orthodox church on Crkvina hill above Trebinje (a 1990s replica of the medieval Gracanica in Kosovo), holding the tomb of poet Jovan Ducic. The terrace gives the best panorama over the old town, the Trebisnjica valley and the mountains.
communityNotes: Consistently named the second Trebinje highlight after the Arslanagic bridge: 'the other highlight is the Serbian Orthodox monastery up on a hill overlooking the town, with a great view down'. Travel reports call the view 'breathtaking'. Short drive up; dress modestly to enter the church.
bestTime: Golden hour for the panorama

name: Vjetrenica Cave (Zavala, near Ravno)
description: The largest and most biodiverse cave in BiH (UNESCO Tentative List), at Zavala near Ravno in the Popovo Polje karst — named 'wind cave' for the strong draught at its mouth. A guided ~45-60 min walk ~600m in past chambers, columns and underground lakes; home to the blind cave salamander (olm). ~1h from Trebinje.
communityNotes: Mixed but mostly positive. Fans: 'spectacular halls', 'what you can see cannot be described', 'easy and fascinating day trip from Dubrovnik'. Differs from show-caves: 'not many stalactites' but unique pools/lakes. Watch-outs: can feel underwhelming if you've seen big caves, and service is hit-or-miss (one praised guide Maria, another slammed a guide on 'auto-mode'). Tours hourly on the hour, mandatory guide, ~12C inside, slippery paths.
cost: Cheap entry (~10 KM / a few euro pp); cash KM safest
timeNeeded: 45-60 min tour; ~1h drive each way from Trebinje
bestTime: Bring a layer (constant ~12C); check last-entry time
facilities: Guided tours only, parking, nearby restaurant for the wait between slots

name: Jablanica Kayak Safari (Neretva canyon)
description: Self-guided kayak rental on the emerald Neretva canyon below Jablanica dam — paddle through sheer canyon walls to a secluded river beach. Staff brief you, kayaks + life jackets + waterproof bags provided; return tow available. 5-star across 60+ reviews, €25pp. On the confirmed route between Mostar and Konjic.
communityNotes: 'Self guided kayak down the canyon, you can go at your own pace and enjoy the stunning scenery'; 'the calm river currents make it easy to paddle with minimal effort'. Red Bull cliff-diver rocks mid-route for optional jumps. Discovered by overlanders 'accidentally while stopping for fuel' — consistently the Bosnia leg surprise. Available without pre-booking in June; open 10:00–19:00.
bestTime: morning, before afternoon wind

name: Dragoš Sedlo viewpoint — Skakavac waterfall (Sutjeska NP)
description: Viewpoint at 1306m on the Dragoš Sedlo plateau inside Sutjeska NP — ~100m walk from a parking area — with a panorama of the 70m Skakavac waterfall and the Perućica primeval forest below. Reached by a ~10km macadam road off the M18 from Tjentište. Viable as a 1.5–2h transit stop on the Sarajevo → Šćepan Polje drive. No guide needed for the viewpoint; guide required only to enter Perućica forest.
communityNotes: A way to 'see Sutjeska without a full day' — the viewpoint gives you the waterfall panorama in ~15min walk. Road is manageable in a normal car (dusty, some rough spots). The full Skakavac hike (2–3h RT, guide mandatory, ~€20–30/group) is beautiful but kills a half-day. | Tjentište monument (WWII Battle of Sutjeska) is on the M18 directly — a free 20-min stop even without the detour.

name: Cinemas Sloga — Monday Latin Night (Sarajevo)
description: Sarajevo's best Monday night — free Latin dance night every Monday at a retro cinema venue on Dalmatinska 36, near Baščaršija. Two-floor, ~500-person 'enormous cavernous club'. Salsa, bachata, merengue. Starts properly at 22:00.
communityNotes: Free every Monday — no cover charge. 'Local crowd, excellent atmosphere, genuinely a Sarajevo institution.' Confirmed at salsavida.com and nightflow.com. Capacity ~500 (not 1,000 — that's Central the Club). Gets going properly around 22:00; ends earlier than weekends. Alternative same-night: **Underground Club** (Ulica Mehmeda Spahe 1) also does Monday Latin from 20:00. Bar crawl pre-warmup: Ferhadija/Baščaršija (€1.50 rakija, €2.30 craft beer).
bestTime: Monday only, starts 22:00

name: Čelebići river swim — Idbar meets Neretva (M-17 roadside stop)
description: Where the clear Idbar tributary meets the emerald Neretva, ~25km north of Mostar on the M-17 Jablanica road. A natural swim pool with a small waterfall next to a roadside restaurant — zero detour, already on the Day 7 Mostar→Konjic road.
communityNotes: Cold river water (12-15°C — Neretva is glacier-fed all summer) but an emerald color that rewards the dip. The Čelebići restaurant next to the confluence has parking, toilets, and cold drinks. Pull off, swim, continue — perfect 30min reset between Mostar and Konjic. More of a 'roadside paradise' than a destination. The M-17 itself has several pull-off gravel banks along Jablaničko Lake further north — locals swim from these on hot days.
bestTime: midday (afternoon gets shade from canyon walls)

name: Kino Bosna (Sarajevo — former cinema bar)
description: Former Austro-Hungarian cinema in Sarajevo's Marijin Dvor district converted into a bar and cultural space — the architecture alone is worth the visit. Cheap food and drinks; live sevdah (Bosnian blues) concerts on Mondays and Thursdays.
communityNotes: One of the best Sarajevo locals' night-out venues: 'architecture alone worth it, live sevdah on Mon/Thu, cheap food and drinks.' Sevdah is the soulful folk music of Bosnia — hearing it live in a place like this is a genuine experience. Not a tourist bar.
bestTime: Monday or Thursday evenings for live sevdah
